statement: Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir presented a bill on 18 September 2025 to implement Protocol 35 of the EEA Agreement into Icelandic law, establishing a limited priority rule for properly transposed EEA obligations. She cited Hæstiréttur's (Supreme Court) ruling of 28 February (case 24/2023, the 'fæðingarorlofsdómur') which confirmed that Protocol 35 had not been properly implemented in Icelandic law, resulting in a young doctor losing maternity leave benefits she would have been entitled to under correctly transposed EEA rules. The bill follows the Norwegian model (EEA Act § 2) and establishes that Icelandic laws implementing EEA obligations take precedence over conflicting domestic legislation, while preserving Alþingi's sovereign right to legislate contrary to EEA rules if it explicitly chooses to do so. She characterised this as protecting individuals' and businesses' rights under Iceland's most important trade agreement, not as a transfer of sovereignty.
source_name: Alþingi — Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir, utanríkisráðherra (flutningsræða, 156. löggjafarþing)
caveats: The bill remained controversial, with opposition arguing it constituted creeping sovereignty transfer. The Supreme Court case (24/2023) is accurately cited but the broader implications of Protocol 35 implementation are debated among Icelandic constitutional scholars. The Norwegian model comparison is relevant but Norway's legal system differs in its approach to international law integration.

related_entries.statement: The EU has adopted a vast body of legislation since its founding. As of end 2024, the EUR-Lex database records approximately 146,700 regulations and 4,600 directives adopted since 1958, totalling over 151,000 binding legal acts. During the EEA period (1994–2024), the EU adopted roughly 57,100 regulations (averaging 1,842 per year) and 2,626 directives (85 per year). Legislative output has declined from a peak of approximately 4,000+ regulations per year in the late 1980s to around 1,200 per year in 2019–2023.

Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU) provides that any member state may decide to withdraw from the Union in accordance with its own constitutional requirements. The withdrawal process involves a two-year negotiation period, extendable by unanimous agreement of the European Council.

As of 2025, approximately 70% of EU internal market legislation has been incorporated into Icelandic law through the EEA Agreement. This covers free movement of goods, services, capital, and persons, but excludes fisheries, agriculture, and justice/home affairs.
